summary: Gruber argues that Twitter made a serious mistake by doubling its character limit from 140 to 280. He curates reactions from notable Twitter users including J.K. Rowling, Stephen King, and Andy Ihnatko, all of whom lament the change. His core argument is that the 140-character constraint was Twitter's unique selling proposition — it forced creativity and brevity, making the platform feel like a fun challenge rather than a chore. By expanding to 280 characters, Twitter made itself more like Facebook, a platform that already exists and serves that purpose.
key_points: The 140-character limit was Twitter's unique selling proposition, and removing it eliminates what made the platform distinctive
Twitter trying to be more like Facebook is a losing strategy — Facebook already occupies that space
The constraint forced creative brevity, making tweeting feel like solving a small puzzle rather than a chore
Given more space, people will use it even when unnecessary, degrading the timeline experience
Writers in particular valued the discipline the character limit imposed
money_quotes: Twitter trying to be more like Facebook is like basketball trying to be more like football — a bad idea that won't work.
By doubling the character limit, Twitter has eliminated what made them unique.
Given 280 characters, people are going to use them, even to express thoughts that could have fit in 140.
Given unlimited characters, such as in email, people ramble aimlessly.
That's why email feels like a dreary chore, and Twitter feels like fun.
The fewer tweets that fit in a single screen at a time, the less fun Twitter feels.
Fitting certain complex thoughts into a mere 140 characters sometimes felt like solving a small challenge, like one of The New York Times's tiny little 5 × 5 crossword puzzles.
key_insight: Constraints breed creativity, and by doubling Twitter's character limit, the platform destroyed the very limitation that made it uniquely compelling.
